36	# Lean Auditor
37	
38	You read **every `.lean` file in the project** and produce a per-file checklist of outdated comments, suspect definitions, dead-end proofs, and bad Lean practices. You are **read-only** and your only writable target is your own report under `task_results/`.
39	
40	You are **highly recommended in the review phase**: the review agent dispatches you whenever any `.lean` file has been modified this iter, OR whenever the prior verdict had live must-fix findings. Your output flags issues the prover and plan agents may have missed.
41	
42	## Stance: no strategic bias
43	
44	You audit the Lean **as Lean**. Your directive will NOT include the project strategy, the blueprint, or "what we are trying to prove" — and that is intentional. The point is to detect mathematically-wrong definitions and dead-end proofs *without* being swayed by what the project assumes should be true. A definition is wrong if it is wrong by reading the Lean alone (e.g. its body contradicts standard mathematical usage of the named concept); you do not need to know the strategy to spot that.
45	
46	This stance is also what makes you **critical of excuse-comments**. Lean-side comments like:
47	
48	- `-- We decided to use a temporary wrong definition; will fix it later.`
49	- `-- This proof is incomplete but works for now.`
50	- `-- TODO: replace before merging.`
51	- `-- Stand-in definition until we figure out the right one.`
52	
53	are RED FLAGS, not project workflow. Authors writing such comments are admitting the code is wrong; the comments cannot be allowed to silence the alarm. Flag every such comment with severity **major** at minimum — and **critical** when the named declaration is load-bearing.
54	
55	## Directive Format
56	
57	```markdown
58	# Lean Auditor Directive
59	
60	## Slug
61	<slug>
62	
63	## Scope (files)
64	<"all" by default. Optionally a list of file paths if the review agent wants to narrow scope; never expand beyond the project.>
65	
66	## Focus areas (optional)
67	<directories or namespaces to pay extra attention to. Bias toward thoroughness — do not skip the rest of the project just because focus is named.>
68	
69	## Known issues
70	<things the review agent already knows and doesn't want re-reported>
71	```
72	
73	The directive **does not** include STRATEGY.md, PROGRESS.md, references, or a description of what the project is trying to prove. That is by design.
74	
75	## What you do
76	
77	1. Read your directive.
78	2. **List every `.lean` file** under the project. Use `find` or `ls -R` — do not rely on import graphs (some files may be temporarily orphaned).
79	3. **For each file** (every one, no scope shortcuts):
80	   - Read the whole file.
81	   - For each declaration: is the signature reasonable as Lean? Is the body suspect (uses `axiom`, `Classical.choice _`, `sorry` with a body that looks like fake content, `:= True`, `:= rfl` on a non-trivial claim)?
82	   - For each comment: is it stale (referencing removed code or old strategies)? Is it an *excuse-comment* (see above)? Is it accurate about the code below it?
83	   - For each proof: is the tactic chain reasonable for the goal type, or does it look like it is going in the wrong direction (e.g. unfolding to manipulate syntax instead of using the available structure lemmas)?
84	   - Note any bad Lean practices: misuse of universe polymorphism, redundant typeclasses, deprecated tactics, anti-patterns like proving `False`-style lemmas to bypass infrastructure.
85	4. **Produce a per-file checklist** + a flagged-issues block grouped by severity.
86	
87	You may use:
88	- `archon-lean-lsp` MCP tools (read-only): hover, signature lookup, diagnostics. Useful to verify a declaration's actual type.
89	- `sorry_analyzer` if available, to get sorry counts per file.
90	- Standard `Read` / `Grep` for code reading.
91	
92	You may NOT modify any project file. Even when a fix is obvious.
93	
94	## Report format
95	
96	Write your report to `.archon/task_results/lean-auditor-<slug>.md` (or the parent-aware path under `task_results/<parent-slug>/` when invoked nested — your invocation prompt names the exact path).

127	
128	## Must-fix-this-iter
129	
130	Apply verbatim. Every one of the following lands here automatically, no exceptions, no under-classification:
131	
132	- **Excuse-comments** on any declaration: `-- TODO replace with real def`, `-- placeholder`, `-- temporary`, `-- wrong but works`, `-- will fix later`. These are admissions that the code is wrong; treat them as wrong.
133	- **Weakened-wrong definitions**: a `def Foo := <something structurally different>` standing in for the real concept (e.g. `Scheme.LineBundle := CommRing.Pic …` when the real definition is an invertible quasi-coherent sheaf).
134	- **Parallel APIs of existing Mathlib**: a project file that copy-and-modifies a Mathlib definition (changing one type-class or argument) instead of instancing the existing Mathlib version. Bridge lemmas needed to consume it are the symptom.
135	- **Suspect bodies on substantive claims**: `:= sorry` on a load-bearing claim, `:= True`, `:= rfl` on a non-trivial statement, `:= Classical.choice _` without authorization.
136	- **Axioms** on non-trivial claims that the project hasn't explicitly authorized in the strategy.

138	A finding that meets any of the above lands at must-fix-this-iter — even if classifying it as "major" would feel more diplomatic. Soft severity is how wrong code hardens into the project.

162	- ...
163	
164	## Severity summary

167	- **major**: <N>
168	- **minor**: <N>
169	- **excuse-comments**: <N> (also counted under must-fix-this-iter above; called out separately because they document the project lying to itself).
170	
171	Overall verdict: one sentence.
172	```

If you under-classify a wrong-decision finding to keep the loop's momentum, you are working against the project — wrong code accumulates faster than reviewers can catch it later. When in doubt, classify higher, not lower.

6

8

23	- **`archon-informal-agent.py`** — call external LLMs (OpenAI/Gemini/OpenRouter) for an informal proof-style sketch when you're stuck on an approach and want a second opinion. **Requires `OPENAI_API_KEY` / `GEMINI_API_KEY` / `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` in env** — check via `env | grep -E "OPENAI|GEMINI|OPENROUTER"` BEFORE planning around its use; if no key is set, fall back to alternatives below. The output is LLM-generated from training data, NOT source-derived: this tool is **not** a literature retriever. For actual literature lookup (cross-check a claim against Hartshorne, fetch an arXiv paper, summarize a Stacks tag) consult the auto-injected subagent catalog — when a literature/reference-fetching subagent is enabled it will appear there — or use the `WebSearch` / `WebFetch` tools directly.
24	- **`archon-subagent.py`** — the generic subagent dispatcher (one wrapper handles every subagent — see "Subagents" below).
25	
26	## Subagents
27	
28	Subagents are descriptor files at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md` (YAML frontmatter — `name`, `description`, `write_domain`, `read_only`, `can_spawn`, `default_enabled`, optional `mandatory: [<phase>...]`, optional `dispatcher_notes` — followed by the prompt body the spawned Claude reads).
29	
30	**You do NOT need to discover subagents.** The plan and review prompts auto-inject an **Available subagents** section at the top of each invocation listing every enabled descriptor with description, write-domain, MANDATORY flag, and `dispatcher_notes`. When you decide to invoke a subagent, read its full prompt at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md`.
31	
32	**Invoke** via the generic wrapper (Bash tool, foreground):
33	
34	```
35	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
36	  --name <subagent-name> \
37	  --slug <kebab-slug> \
38	  --directive-file <path-to-directive.md> \
39	  --write-domain '<glob>'        # repeat for multiple
40	```
41	
42	The wrapper exits 0 on success, prints a one-line status, writes the report to `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`, and the Archon CLI auto-archives a copy to `logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-report.md` for the dashboard. The dispatch semaphore caps concurrent subagent processes at `loop.max_parallel`; the wrapper resolves `--parent-slug` from `ARCHON_SUBAGENT_SLUG` so nested children inherit the hierarchy.
43	
44	**Treat each dispatch as blocking — wait for its report before acting on it.** Don't *deliberately* set `run_in_background: true`. But note the wrapper is genuinely synchronous (it `subprocess.run`s the `archon subagent` CLI and only returns once the child finishes and its report is on disk), so the harness may *auto-background* a long-running dispatch and hand you a task ID immediately — that's expected and fine. When it does, await that task / poll for the report file at `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md` (the wrapper's one-line status and exit 0 confirm completion) before you read or act on the result. The thing to avoid is firing a dispatch and then continuing to plan as if it had already returned; whether the call blocked inline or was auto-backgrounded, the rule is the same: do not consume a subagent's output until its report exists.
45	
46	**Highly-recommended subagents.** A descriptor whose frontmatter sets `mandatory: [plan]` is treated as **highly recommended** for the plan phase (similarly `[review]` for the review phase). The catalog tags them `[HIGHLY RECOMMENDED]`. Dispatch each one unless you have a concrete reason to skip — when skipping, record a one-line rationale under a `## Subagent skips` section in `iter/iter-NNN/<phase>.md` (e.g. `- strategy-critic: STRATEGY.md SHA unchanged from prior iter and last verdict was SOUND`). A post-phase audit checks both signals: it silences when the subagent dispatched OR a skip rationale was recorded, and warns only when neither happened. Each subagent's `dispatcher_notes` enumerates its specific skip conditions — read them before deciding. **Subagents ship disabled by default** (`default_enabled: false`), so the rule only fires for subagents the user has explicitly listed under `subagents.enabled` in `.archon/config.json`; on a fresh project the catalog is empty and no recommended dispatch is required. (The frontmatter field is still named `mandatory: [...]` for backward compat with existing configs and descriptors — its semantic meaning is "highly recommended", not "must dispatch".)

71	### Subagent permissions
72	
73	Each subagent ships YAML frontmatter:
74	
75	- `write_domain: "<glob>"` — file pattern the subagent may write. Empty for purely read-only subagents (the dispatching call typically passes `--write-domain 'task_results/**'` so the subagent can still write its own report).
76	- `read_only: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may only write its `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`.
77	- `can_spawn: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may dispatch children whose `write_domain` is a subset of its own.

To invoke a refactor-style subagent inside the loop, write the directive to a fresh tempfile under `.archon/logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-directive.md` and dispatch via the generic wrapper. Older state files (`STRATEGY.md`, `task_pending.md`, `PROGRESS.md`) may contain leftover references to the old REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md flow — treat those as historical noise.

They may NEVER rename / re-type / delete / re-sign a protected declaration.
89	- The YAML file itself is user-only. No agent adds or removes a declaration. The only allowed agent edit is updating an existing declaration's file path.

91	## Blueprint Marker Vocabulary
92	
93	Two semantic markers:
94	
95	- `\leanok` — statement block: declaration is formalized (at least a `sorry` present). Proof block: proof closed, no `sorry`.
96	- `\mathlibok` — statement block: declaration is backed by Mathlib (re-export / alias); no Archon proof obligation remains.
97	
98	No marker means the block is unformalized. If a block fails to translate, leave it unmarked and annotate with a `% NOTE:` comment.
99	
100	**`\leanok` is managed deterministically by the `sync_leanok` phase** that runs between prover and review each iteration. It walks every chapter, looks up each `\lean{...}` declaration, runs `sorry_analyzer` + `lake env lean`, and adds/removes `\leanok` accordingly. Agents must NOT add or remove `\leanok` themselves.

6	import AlgebraicJacobian.Genus
7	
8	/-!
9	# Abelian-variety rigidity: morphisms from a genus-`0` curve into an abelian variety are constant
10	
11	This file scaffolds the project's **committed characteristic-free** genus-`0` route
12	(route (c)): the abelian-variety rigidity stack. It sits **upstream** of
13	`AlgebraicJacobian.Jacobian` (it imports only `AlgebraicJacobian.Genus`), breaking the
14	`RigidityKbar → Rigidity → Jacobian` import cycle so that `genusZeroWitness` can consume the
15	genus-`0` rigidity keystone directly.
16	
17	The headline `rigidity_genus0_curve_to_grpScheme` is the char-free replacement (no
18	`[CharZero kbar]`) for `AlgebraicGeometry.rigidity_over_kbar` of `AlgebraicJacobian.RigidityKbar`
19	(which remains in tree as the fallback route (a) artifact and still carries `[CharZero]`).
20	
21	The minimal chain has four links:
22	
23	1. `rigidity_lemma` — the Rigidity Lemma (Mumford, Form I): the cube-free, cohomology-free
24	   properness/closed-map entry point. **Proven** (iter-157–159) modulo the single residual
25	   geometric helper `rigidity_eqOn_saturated_open_to_affine` (bridge 2, the slice-constancy /
26	   affine-constancy equation), which is the lone `sorry` of the Rigidity-Lemma chain.
27	2. `morphism_P1_to_grpScheme_const` — every morphism `ℙ¹ → A` into an abelian variety is
28	   constant (blocked downstream on the theorem of the cube).
29	3. `genusZero_curve_iso_P1` — a smooth proper geom-irred genus-`0` curve over `k̄` is
30	   isomorphic to `ℙ¹` (blocked on Riemann–Roch).
31	4. `rigidity_genus0_curve_to_grpScheme` — THE HEADLINE consumed by `genusZeroWitness`.
32	
33	See `blueprint/src/chapters/AbelianVarietyRigidity.tex` for the informal sketches and sources
34	(Mumford, *Abelian Varieties*, Ch. II §4, §6; Milne, *Abelian Varieties*, Prop. 3.10;
35	Hartshorne, *Algebraic Geometry*, Example IV.1.3.5).
36	
37	## Encoding notes (iter-157 scaffold)
38	
39	Mathlib `b80f227` packages no `ℙ¹` as a `Scheme`, so — following the established project idiom
40	(see `AlgebraicJacobian.RigidityKbar`) — the projective line is encoded by its abstract
41	characterisation: a smooth proper geometrically irreducible `Over (Spec (.of kbar))` of relative
42	dimension `1` with `genus = 0`. The signatures of declarations 1–3 are **provisional**
43	(`SCAFFOLD` comments mark them); the prover may refine the encoding when the bodies are filled.
44	Declaration 4 is pinned verbatim to `rigidity_over_kbar`'s signature minus `[CharZero kbar]`,
45	because it is the exact signature the consumer (`genusZeroWitness.key`) needs.
46	-/
47	
48	set_option autoImplicit false
49	
50	universe u
51	
52	open CategoryTheory Limits MonoidalCategory CartesianMonoidalCategory MonObj
53	
54	namespace AlgebraicGeometry
55	
56	variable {kbar : Type u} [Field kbar]
57	
58	/-- **Cartesian-monoidal identity (skeleton step of the Rigidity Lemma).** Post-composing the
59	second projection `snd : X ⊗ Y ⟶ Y` with the slice section `y ↦ (x₀, y)` is the "collapse the
60	`X`-axis onto `x₀`" endomorphism `(x, y) ↦ (x₀, y)` of `X ⊗ Y`:
61	`snd ≫ lift (toUnit Y ≫ x₀) (𝟙 Y) = lift (toUnit (X ⊗ Y) ≫ x₀) (snd X Y)`.
62	
63	Pure cartesian-monoidal algebra (no geometry): `comp_lift` distributes the `snd`, the
64	`𝟙 Y` component simplifies by `Category.comp_id`, and the `toUnit Y` component collapses by
65	uniqueness of maps into the terminal object. -/
66	theorem rigidity_snd_lift
67	    {X Y : Over (Spec (.of kbar))}
68	    (x₀ : 𝟙_ (Over (Spec (.of kbar))) ⟶ X) :
69	    snd X Y ≫ lift (toUnit Y ≫ x₀) (𝟙 Y) =
70	      lift (toUnit (X ⊗ Y) ≫ x₀) (snd X Y) := by
71	  ext1 <;> simp
72	
73	/-- **Bridge 1 of the Rigidity Lemma (closed-map step), PROVEN.** When `X` is complete (proper)
74	over `k̄`, the second monoidal projection `snd : X ⊗ Y ⟶ Y` has, on underlying schemes, a
75	*closed* base map. This is Mumford's "completeness of `X` makes `p₂` a closed map" (Abelian
76	Varieties, Ch. II §4, p. 43).
77	
78	Proof: the underlying scheme morphism `(snd X Y).left` is the pullback projection
79	`Limits.pullback.snd X.hom Y.hom` (`Over.snd_left`), i.e. the base change of `X.hom` along
80	`Y.hom`. `IsProper X.hom ⟹ UniversallyClosed X.hom` (`IsProper.toUniversallyClosed`), and
81	`UniversallyClosed` is stable under base change
82	(`universallyClosed_isStableUnderBaseChange.of_isPullback` on the canonical pullback square), so
83	`(snd X Y).left` is universally closed and hence its base map is closed
84	(`Scheme.Hom.isClosedMap`). Char-free; no theorem of the cube, no cohomology. -/
85	theorem snd_left_isClosedMap
86	    {X Y : Over (Spec (.of kbar))} [IsProper X.hom] :
87	    IsClosedMap (snd X Y).left.base := by
88	  haveI hp : UniversallyClosed X.hom := IsProper.toUniversallyClosed
89	  haveI : UniversallyClosed (snd X Y).left := by
90	    rw [Over.snd_left]
91	    exact universallyClosed_isStableUnderBaseChange.of_isPullback
92	      (IsPullback.of_hasPullback X.hom Y.hom) hp
93	  exact Scheme.Hom.isClosedMap _
94	
95	/-- **Dense-closed-points hom-extensionality (the bespoke globalisation connective, PROVEN).**
96	Two morphisms `g₁ g₂ : W ⟶ Z` out of a *reduced* scheme `W` whose closed points are *dense*
97	(`[JacobsonSpace W]` — e.g. when `W` is locally of finite type over a field) into a *separated*
98	scheme `Z` are equal as soon as they agree at every closed point `x ∈ closedPoints W` after the
99	canonical residue-field probe `W.fromSpecResidueField x : Spec κ(x) ⟶ W`.
100	
101	This is the one connective the iter-159 `mathlib-analogist` flagged that Mathlib does **not**
102	package directly: Mathlib supplies only the single-dominant-morphism `ext_of_isDominant`. Here we
103	assemble all the closed points into one dominant probe — the coproduct
104	`∐_{x ∈ closedPoints W} Spec κ(x) ⟶ W`, whose topological range is exactly the (dense) set of
105	closed points — and feed it to `ext_of_isDominant`. It is `Step 2` of bridge 2's route B
106	(cohomology-free) and is fully proven here, reusable independently of the rigidity context. -/
107	theorem morphism_eq_of_eqAt_closedPoints
108	    {W Z : Scheme.{u}} [IsReduced W] [JacobsonSpace W] [Z.IsSeparated]
109	    {g₁ g₂ : W ⟶ Z}
110	    (h : ∀ x ∈ closedPoints W,
111	      W.fromSpecResidueField x ≫ g₁ = W.fromSpecResidueField x ≫ g₂) :
112	    g₁ = g₂ := by
113	  -- The dominant probe: the coproduct of the residue-field `Spec`s over the closed points.
114	  let F : closedPoints W → Scheme.{u} := fun x => Spec (W.residueField x.1)
115	  let probe : (∐ F) ⟶ W := Sigma.desc fun x => W.fromSpecResidueField x.1
116	  -- Its topological range contains every closed point, hence (Jacobson) is dense.
117	  haveI : IsDominant probe := by
118	    refine ⟨(dense_iff_closure_eq.mpr (closure_closedPoints (X := W))).mono ?_⟩
119	    intro x hx
120	    obtain ⟨pt⟩ : Nonempty (Spec (W.residueField x)) := inferInstance
121	    refine ⟨(Sigma.ι F ⟨x, hx⟩).base pt, ?_⟩
122	    have hcomp : Sigma.ι F ⟨x, hx⟩ ≫ probe = W.fromSpecResidueField x := Sigma.ι_desc _ _
123	    have e1 : probe.base ((Sigma.ι F ⟨x, hx⟩).base pt) = (W.fromSpecResidueField x).base pt := by
124	      rw [← Scheme.Hom.comp_apply, hcomp]
125	    rw [e1]
126	    exact Set.eq_of_mem_singleton (Scheme.range_fromSpecResidueField x ▸ Set.mem_range_self pt)
127	  -- Componentwise the probe equalises `g₁` and `g₂`; dominance then forces `g₁ = g₂`.
128	  refine ext_of_isDominant probe (Sigma.hom_ext _ _ fun x => ?_)
129	  rw [← Category.assoc, ← Category.assoc, Sigma.ι_desc]
130	  exact h x.1 x.2
131	
132	/-- **Per-closed-slice constancy (Step 1 of bridge 2's route B), the residual deep geometry.**
133	With the data of `rigidity_eqOn_saturated_open_to_affine`, fix a *closed* point `x` of the
134	`p₂`-saturated open `U ⊆ X ⊗ Y` on which `f` lands in the affine `U₀`. Then `f` and the collapsed
135	map `retract ≫ f` (`retract := lift (toUnit (X ⊗ Y) ≫ x₀) (snd X Y)`, i.e. `(x,y) ↦ (x₀,y)`)
136	agree at `x` after the residue-field probe `U.fromSpecResidueField x`.
137	
138	Mumford's "for each `y ∈ V`, the complete slice `X × {y}` maps into the affine, hence to a single

402	## Mumford's proof of `rigidity_eqOn_dense_open` (Abelian Varieties, Ch. II §4, p. 43)
403	
404	Set `g(y) = f(x₀, y)` (so `retract ≫ f` is exactly `(x, y) ↦ g(y)`). The open `V` is produced as
405	follows: let `U` be an affine open neighbourhood of a chosen point in `Z`, `F = Z ∖ U`, and
406	`G = (snd X Y) '' (f ⁻¹ F)`. Then for each `y ∈ V := Y ∖ G`, the slice `f(X × {y}) ⊆ U`, and `f`
407	agrees with `retract ≫ f` on the non-empty open `X × V`.
408	
409	This rests on **two char-free Mathlib bridges** (the cube-free heart); bridge 1 is now **built**,
410	bridge 2 is the lone residual `sorry` (isolated in `rigidity_eqOn_saturated_open_to_affine`):
411	
412	  1. **Properness ⇒ the projection is a closed map — BUILT** (`snd_left_isClosedMap`, iter-158).
413	     `IsProper X.hom` is universally closed (`AlgebraicGeometry.IsProper.toUniversallyClosed`), and
414	     `snd X Y` is the base change of `X.hom` along `Y.hom`, hence universally closed, hence a closed
415	     map. The glue identifying the monoidal `snd X Y` in `Over (Spec k̄)` with the scheme-theoretic
416	     pullback projection `Limits.pullback.snd X.hom Y.hom` is the exact rewrite `Over.snd_left`, and
417	     `IsClosedMap` is transported across the canonical pullback square via
418	     `universallyClosed_isStableUnderBaseChange`. So `G = snd '' (closed)` is closed, `V` open.
419
